examples.title: Image Classification with CNN on CIFAR-10
examples.description: This demonstrates building a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) from scratch to classify images into 10 categories using the CIFAR-10 dataset. It covers basic CNN architecture with convolutional, pooling, and fully connected layers.
examples.code:
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow.keras import datasets, layers, models
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Load CIFAR-10 dataset
(train_images, train_labels), (test_images, test_labels) = datasets.cifar10.load_data()

# Normalize pixel values to [0, 1]
train_images, test_images = train_images / 255.0, test_images / 255.0

# Define class names
class_names = ['airplane', 'automobile', 'bird', 'cat', 'deer',
               'dog', 'frog', 'horse', 'ship', 'truck']

# Build CNN model
model = models.Sequential([
    # First convolutional block
    layers.Conv2D(32, (3, 3), activation='relu', input_shape=(32, 32, 3)),
    layers.MaxPooling2D((2, 2)),
    
    # Second convolutional block
    layers.Conv2D(64, (3, 3), activation='relu'),
    layers.MaxPooling2D((2, 2)),
    
    # Third convolutional block
    layers.Conv2D(64, (3, 3), activation='relu'),
    
    # Dense layers
    layers.Flatten(),
    layers.Dense(64, activation='relu'),
    layers.Dropout(0.5),
    layers.Dense(10)
])

# Compile and train
model.compile(optimizer='adam',
              loss=tf.keras.losses.SparseCategoricalCrossentropy(from_logits=True),
              metrics=['accuracy'])

history = model.fit(train_images, train_labels, epochs=10,
                    validation_data=(test_images, test_labels))

# Evaluate model
test_loss, test_acc = model.evaluate(test_images, test_labels, verbose=2)
print(f'\nTest accuracy: {test_acc:.4f}')
examples.explanation: 1. Load CIFAR-10 dataset containing 50,000 training and 10,000 test images across 10 categories. 2. Normalize pixel values to 0-1 range for better gradient flow. 3. Build a Sequential CNN with 3 convolutional blocks (Conv2D + MaxPooling) for feature extraction, followed by Dense layers for classification. 4. Add Dropout (0.5) to prevent overfitting. 5. Compile with Adam optimizer and SparseCategoricalCrossentropy loss (for integer labels). 6. Train for 10 epochs and evaluate on test set.

examples.title: Object Detection with Pre-trained YOLOv8
examples.description: This demonstrates real-time object detection using YOLOv8 (You Only Look Once), a state-of-the-art pre-trained model that can detect multiple objects in images and video streams with bounding boxes and class labels.
examples.code:
# Install required packages: pip install ultralytics opencv-python

from ultralytics import YOLO
import cv2
import numpy as np

# Load pre-trained YOLOv8 model (nano version for speed)
model = YOLO('yolov8n.pt')

# Load and preprocess image
image_path = 'sample_image.jpg'
results = model(image_path, save=True, conf=0.25, iou=0.7)

# Access detection results
for result in results:
    boxes = result.boxes
    print(f"Detected {len(boxes)} objects")
    
    # Iterate through detections
    for box in boxes:
        # Get bounding box coordinates (xyxy format)
        x1, y1, x2, y2 = box.xyxy[0].cpu().numpy()
        
        # Get confidence score
        conf = float(box.conf[0])
        
        # Get class ID and name
        class_id = int(box.cls[0])
        class_name = model.names[class_id]
        
        print(f"  {class_name}: {conf:.2f} at [{x1:.0f}, {y1:.0f}, {x2:.0f}, {y2:.0f}]")

# Real-time detection from webcam
# Uncomment below for live detection:
# cap = cv2.VideoCapture(0)
# while cap.isOpened():
#     ret, frame = cap.read()
#     if not ret:
#         break
#     results = model(frame, conf=0.25)
#     annotated_frame = results[0].plot()
#     cv2.imshow('YOLO Detection', annotated_frame)
#     if cv2.waitKey(1) & 0xFF == ord('q'):
#         break
# cap.release()
# cv2.destroyAllWindows()
examples.explanation: 1. Import YOLO from ultralytics and OpenCV for image processing. 2. Load pre-trained YOLOv8n (nano) model - smallest and fastest variant. 3. Run inference on image with confidence threshold (0.25) and IoU threshold (0.7) for Non-Maximum Suppression. 4. Extract detection results: bounding box coordinates (xyxy), confidence scores, class IDs, and class names. 5. The model can detect 80 different object classes (COCO dataset). 6. For real-time detection, use OpenCV to capture webcam frames and process each frame through YOLO.

examples.title: Image Segmentation with U-Net for Medical Imaging
examples.description: This demonstrates semantic image segmentation using U-Net architecture, commonly used in medical imaging for tasks like tumor detection, cell segmentation, and organ delineation. The example builds a complete U-Net from scratch for binary segmentation.
examples.code:
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow.keras import layers, models
import numpy as np

# U-Net Architecture for Image Segmentation
def unet_model(input_size=(256, 256, 1)):
    inputs = layers.Input(input_size)
    
    # Encoder (Contracting Path)
    # Block 1
    c1 = layers.Conv2D(64, 3, activation='relu', padding='same')(inputs)
    c1 = layers.Conv2D(64, 3, activation='relu', padding='same')(c1)
    c1 = layers.BatchNormalization()(c1)
    p1 = layers.MaxPooling2D((2, 2))(c1)
    p1 = layers.Dropout(0.1)(p1)
    
    # Block 2
    c2 = layers.Conv2D(128, 3, activation='relu', padding='same')(p1)
    c2 = layers.Conv2D(128, 3, activation='relu', padding='same')(c2)
    c2 = layers.BatchNormalization()(c2)
    p2 = layers.MaxPooling2D((2, 2))(c2)
    p2 = layers.Dropout(0.1)(p2)
    
    # Block 3
    c3 = layers.Conv2D(256, 3, activation='relu', padding='same')(p2)
    c3 = layers.Conv2D(256, 3, activation='relu', padding='same')(c3)
    c3 = layers.BatchNormalization()(c3)
    p3 = layers.MaxPooling2D((2, 2))(c3)
    p3 = layers.Dropout(0.2)(p3)
    
    # Block 4 (Bottleneck)
    c4 = layers.Conv2D(512, 3, activation='relu', padding='same')(p3)
    c4 = layers.Conv2D(512, 3, activation='relu', padding='same')(c4)
    c4 = layers.BatchNormalization()(c4)
    
    # Decoder (Expanding Path)
    # Block 5
    u5 = layers.Conv2DTranspose(256, 2, strides=(2, 2), padding='same')(c4)
    u5 = layers.concatenate([u5, c3])
    u5 = layers.Dropout(0.2)(u5)
    c5 = layers.Conv2D(256, 3, activation='relu', padding='same')(u5)
    c5 = layers.Conv2D(256, 3, activation='relu', padding='same')(c5)
    
    # Block 6
    u6 = layers.Conv2DTranspose(128, 2, strides=(2, 2), padding='same')(c5)
    u6 = layers.concatenate([u6, c2])
    u6 = layers.Dropout(0.1)(u6)
    c6 = layers.Conv2D(128, 3, activation='relu', padding='same')(u6)
    c6 = layers.Conv2D(128, 3, activation='relu', padding='same')(c6)
    
    # Block 7
    u7 = layers.Conv2DTranspose(64, 2, strides=(2, 2), padding='same')(c6)
    u7 = layers.concatenate([u7, c1])
    u7 = layers.Dropout(0.1)(u7)
    c7 = layers.Conv2D(64, 3, activation='relu', padding='same')(u7)
    c7 = layers.Conv2D(64, 3, activation='relu', padding='same')(c7)
    
    # Output layer (binary segmentation)
    outputs = layers.Conv2D(1, 1, activation='sigmoid')(c7)
    
    model = models.Model(inputs, outputs)
    return model

# Create model
model = unet_model(input_size=(256, 256, 1))

# Compile with Dice loss for better segmentation
def dice_loss(y_true, y_pred):
    smooth = 1e-6
    intersection = tf.reduce_sum(y_true * y_pred)
    return 1 - (2. * intersection + smooth) / (tf.reduce_sum(y_true) + tf.reduce_sum(y_pred) + smooth)

model.compile(optimizer='adam', loss=dice_loss, metrics=['accuracy'])
model.summary()

# Example training call (with dummy data)
# Assuming you have images and masks ready
# train_images = np.random.rand(100, 256, 256, 1)
# train_masks = np.random.rand(100, 256, 256, 1)
# model.fit(train_images, train_masks, epochs=50, batch_size=8, validation_split=0.2)
examples.explanation: 1. U-Net architecture consists of encoder (contracting path) and decoder (expanding path) connected by skip connections. 2. Encoder: 4 blocks with Conv2D layers for feature extraction, followed by MaxPooling for downsampling. Each block doubles filters (64→128→256→512). 3. Bottleneck: Deepest layer with maximum feature extraction. 4. Decoder: Uses Conv2DTranspose for upsampling, concatenates with corresponding encoder features (skip connections), and progressively reduces filters. 5. Output: Single channel with sigmoid activation for binary pixel-wise classification (mask or no mask). 6. Uses BatchNormalization for training stability and Dropout for regularization. 7. Custom Dice Loss is used instead of BCE as it's more suitable for imbalanced segmentation tasks.
